speaker: interviewer
text: Gisela Konopka. Tape number eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Paul got, finally, the visa, too, and it is about a month after that I am in the United States. And his story is-- again, that should be separately told someday, because they go on a boat in Marseilles-- I will only say that quickly. They had no idea. They were a whole bunch of refugees, but they had no idea that a whole bunch of Nazis was on that boat, too. And that they, Nazis, had decided that when they come to-- and I forget now the name of the island-- that they will then get all these refugees and kill them. And on the way, while they were on the high seas, a Dutch boat under English command bombs that boat. The German-- the Nazis throw everything that they have into the water because they where the enemies. The Dutch capture the boat, and there are all these refugees, and there are all these Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the Dutch-- no. The refugees said, this is terrible. How do we communicate? But thank god that Paul knows this strange, low German. And so he could explain who they are were.

speaker: interviewee
text: They get them to Trinidad, and they were supposed to. and they were interned in Trinidad. But they wanted to put them all into one internment camp. And Paul went with two others and said, if you do that, and tomorrow morning there are only cut throats. We will not stay one night with these people. They either cut ours or we cut theirs. So they were fortunately separated. Meanwhile, in New York, the embassy heard about all that, and I forgot the name of the American embassy at that time. And people from the [INAUDIBLE]-- they are very good people there who did a lot of work on getting that straightened out. And so finally, they came over. And one day, Paul appeared with the boat. And I knew that they would come, and I was at the boat. And he looked thin and brown. And I thought, I have to give him right away something to eat. He said, no, no, no, they fed us like crazy on that American boat that got us from Trinidad to here. They didn't know, when they saw the menu, that you choose something. So they ate all of this-- everything. Everything. And they fed them that because these were all starving people. So just [INAUDIBLE]. Anyhow, Paul is in New York. And I said, in this country, we can get married in, I thought, six days or three days. Anyhow, on June 23rd-- and I wrote that down, and I wished I could read it, but I won't-- It was very touching because we had no money. The man for whom I worked there was very nice. He said I could work only half a day, you know, and then get married after that half-day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, with that dress? And I said, it's my best dress, you know? So anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to Paul, you know, there's only one thing in America-- they always wear hats now. I don't like hats. So these men had gone out and found a place where I could get a hat for $0.25.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we went to the subway. I said, I don't want to get married without flowers. And they were not my favorite flowers, but there were some bachelor buttons, so I had those. And we went to the-- in New York, it was [FRENCH]. What is it? Oh, that's French. You know. Justice of the peace?

speaker: interviewee
text: Justice of the peace. But on the way, in the subway, Paul said, how do I know when to say yes? We didn't know that one says 'I do.' We thought you just say 'yes.' And I said, well they told me because we don't have any rings that I can hold your hand and I'll press your hand, and you have to say yes. Because he didn't know one word English. Well, that man, he spoke so fast that I hardly could understand. Also, I was very nervous. So I pressed, apparently, his hand all the time. And he said 'yes' I don't know how often. And it was very interesting. And at the end, this wonderful Clark, Mr. Clark, Edwin Clark, who was our landlord, had come as our witness. And there was one Austrian witness. And I know-- I have to think of Goetz. G-O-E-T-Z. And they were our witnesses, and they explained it to the judge, and then the judge became very nice. Very kind. We were very surprised that he was a real human being. That was very nice. They took us out. We ate the cheapest thing on the menu because we didn't dare let anybody spend money on us. So I still remember our wedding was Spanish omelette. And then they took us on a $0.10 ferry, and we go to Staten Island, which was pretty exciting. And we really saw something. We hadn't seen anything before. And then we had our own room for a little bit. Now, this is when we got married. Now, after eight years of almost constant separation, we finally got married. Then the committee, which was, I think, a very good thing-- and I can't say who it was, but the man from-- I think it was the Jewish Refugee Committee-- once spoke to a whole bunch of people and said, don't all stay in New York. This is a big country. It's not a good idea to stay here. We can help you under what's called a resettlement movement. And we can help with putting you somewhere else. Now, meanwhile I had to change committees because Paul was not considered Jewish. So it didn't matter, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: To which committee did you change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a general refugee committee. You know, they were for all the refugees. There were another committee. Just another refugee committee. And we checked what one could do. And somebody-- Maria Halberstadt, H-A-L-B-E-R-S-T-A-D-T, who, in Germany, really was Clara Deppe, D-E-P-P-E, had been much earlier come to the United States. Knew a lot. Worked with all of the peace organizations, and so on. Was a wonderful person. And she had checked [? out. ?] And she said that she thought I should go into social work. Now, I wasn't interested in social work. I was interested in working with young people. But there is a specialization that, unfortunately-- social work has lost a lot. And that was called social group work. Was working in settlement houses, was working with youth-serving organizations. She said you would be wonderful with that. And there is a new school in Pittsburgh, and they are having scholarships, and we can look into that. So committee looked into that and thought I could probably get one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1941. No-- yeah. Wait a minute-- 1941. Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] is still not in the war. It gets into the war in December, '41. Yeah, it's 1941. And so they contact the family service agency in Pittsburgh, and they say the man is a welder, because that's what Paul was willing to do, and he was a good welder. So that's just right. We are an industrial city, she can probably go to school. Paul said fine. We had $5-- [INAUDIBLE] $5 were [INAUDIBLE] this thing. We had $5 in between us two. We got on a bus. I always get deathly sick on a bus, but that's what we did. We went from Pittsburgh-- no, from New York to Pittsburgh. There was a woman who should be enshrined. Her name was Glick. Oh, what's her first name. I forgot, G-L-I-C-K. 50 years later, I hear that her son is here, but she is not alive anymore. Ruth Glick. And Ruth Glick was one of the best social workers I would ever know. She was so immediately-- she said, we can find a place for you. I give you a choice. Either a place where they speak German totally or a place where they speak only English or a place where you can, perhaps, speak both. We said only English. Otherwise, we will never learn it. And so she took us there. And it was one room, and I have pictures of that. And with the widow of a coal miner. And well, that was pure heaven. And that was, first of all, a place to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then she said we should make a-- ah, this is wonderful. We should make a budget. We think that's the funniest thing. When you don't have money, how do you make a budget? You live on what you have. So she says no, no, no, we want to know because our committee will help you in the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we made-- we wrote down so much for rent, so much-- and I never forget, when she took it she says, there's nothing about recreation in that. And we started to laugh. If you don't have money, you don't take money for recreation. That's impossible. We can walk. So these are the kind of nice things. There are-- there's something that was not good, but Ruth, again, was superb. We immediately, almost, were visited by-- I think it must have been a fundamentalist Baptist, or something like that. Very rigid minister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, oh you are refugees? Well, we will take care of you. You know, come to our church. I said no, I'm Jewish, and Paul said, I'm not going to any church. I don't belong to any church.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's impossible. You will never make any living in the United States if you don't belong to a church. Well, we thought, another one who wants to push us into what we don't want.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we told that Ruth Glick. She said, that man will hear from me tomorrow. He's never coming to visit you again. And he didn't. So I think these are the pros and cons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul-- I went with him through the streets. He went into one factory after another with his lousy English, whether he could get work. But he finally found something. I was interviewed and got into the school of social work. They took my credentials as having finished college. This was graduate work. And that was very nice. And we began to feel very good with people because Pittsburgh had some very good union people. This is the time-- again, names. But I think the name was Limbaugh. They were also Jewish, but they were very strong union people, and we became very close friends with them. And they took us, you know, for dinner or something. I mean, into their house. Going out eating is only very recently. So we became friends with quite a lot of people. I started very soon at the school of social work. I sometimes think it's very funny because half of the time, you don't understand what they say when they talk so fast, but it was partially possible. There was one who came from-- I had one professor who came from New York. I always thought he swallowed half of the sentences. I couldn't hear. But somehow you make it. Paul was very, very delighted with the union. There's somebody, [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE]. Well, he was really feeling at home with them. He was just, like, part of them. It was like a wonderful relief. And they were so interested in what we had experienced under the Nazis. In the streets, I was worried sometimes because we met many who didn't think the Nazis were bad at all. They were not necessarily anti-Semites, either. They're just, oh, they're nice, you know. Germany has [INAUDIBLE] food now. You know, they swallowed that propaganda. It was just awful. The same as I had experienced in Austria, when it swallowed propaganda. Propaganda can be very insidious. And so-- but then the war breaks out in December, and Paul immediately says, I'm going back. I fight the Nazis to the last end. Now, you know that everybody gets inducted in the army in the United States. Some people don't know that. You can say no, but then that's it. But Paul wanted to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a few months pass. And again, I'm not going into all the detail with us. He still speaks very poor English. He was very worried about that when he got into the induction center.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came back all elated. They said, you speak better than some of the people who come here. [LAUGHS] That was very funny. But it was sad, that after eight years of separation. And so he went into the army again. And I, meanwhile, studied. And I make that now as short as I can. I have very close friends, even from that period that I was studying. It was a wonderful bunch of people. Jews, non-Jews, and lots of blacks and white. And certainly I was-- immediately, to me, the racial problem in the United States was what I had lived under the Nazis. So we became very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it different for you to meet a black American person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was so imbued that these are people just like I. In fact, I can't help it. I once made a joke to one. I said, this is unbelievable. What's the matter with them? They lie around there in the sun, wanting to get brown and brown and brown, and then they throw you out. And I said they're nuts. I still remember. We laughed. But no, I knew the seriousness of it. And I very quick became part of the Urban League. And, you know we were a wonderful-- certainly I can-- Gertrude Wilson was our professor. She was extraordinary. But think, like, Langston Hughes-- that is a name-- came to lecture to us. And I have a book upstairs that Langston Hughes has signed for Paul and me. I mean, no. This was continuing the fight, only in another way. There's less fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to Langston Hughes then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, but not much. He was a famous man and I was a little student. But he wrote that in the book, and that was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meantime, Paul is in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Paul is in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first it was Fort Bragg. And he was in the gasoline supply, which was interesting enough because it was black and white together, but they were not integrated, and he was fighting on that already at that time. And he became friends with a lot of them. They had lots of stories about Paul in the army that I will not-- but it's fascinating what he did. He-- first, Fort Bragg, and then he was sent to Pittsburgh, California. Now I was in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania and he in Pittsburgh, California. And he said you [? can't ?] come because I'm stationed here. They will send us out to the Japanese part of the war, which is terrible. Really crazy. But he was there. I visited him in camp. Friend of mine, one of the social work colleagues, gave me the money for the train, and I traveled in a train, day and night-- I think four or five days and nights-- from Pittsburgh to Pittsburgh. And I have always said, that's the only way somebody will ever find how big this country is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you to experience that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amazing. First of all, this is war time. We are all pressed into those cars. You know, friends of mine had told me that I must take a dozen boiled eggs and two breads, which I did. And that's what I had. And you didn't go and eat somewhere, you know. But people were very friendly to each other because most of us were army wives, so we were very friendly to each other. But the incredible thing-- I'll never forget. I said, my God, this is a big country. You go over Utah and you cross the Salt Lake, and I just couldn't get over that. And the mountains. And then you land finally in California, feeling awful dirty and awful exhausted. And there is this nice-- they did that for the soldier wives, whatever that was called. I still remember they said, get undressed and iron your dress. It's nicer to meet him with an ironed dress. So I did that. And then I finally saw Paul. And he said he doesn't understand it. A lot of the men from his company were already sent out, and he was always held back. And he didn't understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was there, and we had some very good days together. These I remember, but I could talk a long time about it. It was very special. And the point was that he finally found out they were holding him because there had come news from Washington. They did the dumbest things with soldiers always, you know? Put them into impossible places.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there had been come from Washington that they should hold him because they wanted him for the Office of Strategic Services. To go overseas. So that we didn't know at the time, but finally, the news came. And interesting enough, they came the day when I had to return to Pittsburgh. And if you believe, in filled cars, we both, just arms in arms, could ride back that whole, long trip from there to Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Together. And this is unbelievable. And then he had a few days. It was winter. He had permission to stay, I think, three days. And then he went on to Washington. Now, again, I'm jumping. I didn't know for what he was doing in Washington. But then at one point, he said he will get a few days before he goes overseas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went, again by train, from Pittsburgh to Washington. And I come at the station and there is Paul in civilian clothes. And I said, oh my god, did you run away from the army? You can't do that. Don't be silly. This is because I'm with the Office of Strategic Services.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we learned something that made you see that, to me, in the United States, the fight against race prejudice is related to the black more. Some also to American Indian, but a lot to the black. When we were in Washington, we had a few days, and we wanted to go near the water because we are what we call water rats. We like to swim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we looked for someplace where we could stay. And everywhere it said 'restricted.' And we didn't know what it means. I asked somebody, I said, does it mean restricted to people who have money? No, no. To Jews and niggers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I said, Paul, here we are fighting it over there, and here it is again. Fortunately, we knew some other people who thought differently, but it was horrible. And finally, we found a place, and it was in Virginia. When we walked around and began talking with blacks, we knew was that it wasn't that simple. But we had a few days before he left. [INAUDIBLE] before a soldier left for the overseas, he became a citizen, which was wonderful. And which means that I, too, became much earlier a citizen. I didn't have to wait five years. I think I waited only two or three years. I have my papers here. So that was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he go when he went--

speaker: interviewee
text: London.

speaker: interviewer
text: --back to Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was at London at the labor desk. And constantly in contact with what's going on in Germany. And among the papers that I told you I never opened-- I have them here-- he sent me material that he found in German newspapers that is the most dirty, racial against blacks that you can find.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, I can show it to you. It's unbelievable. But just that-- I will never understand how any Jew can ever be against blacks, you know? I just don't understand that. That we don't know that this is the same. How long was he in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all that time. And he was during all the blitz in London. And I was, meanwhile, working at the child guidance clinic. I began to write, sometimes, about children in wartime and things like that. I mean, I have to say that I finished my studies. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- oh, people don't know how we lived when we were students. I think I should say that. In one room with one girl in one large bed. And it wasn't because we were lesbians, but we had no money to get into any other place. And we lived on soups. So I mean, people should know that. To think that everything comes right like that, you know? You graduated, therefore, with a doctorate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or Masters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, at all. Master's degree. A master's degree in social work in 1943. And I worked at the child guidance clinic, and I started a new program with groups, and that's the way I wrote a lot about it and all that kind of stuff. Now, I have to do this very fast. Paul is still in the army. And then one time in the child guidance clinic-- I think it's 1945-- when I worked with the children, that was done in the basement, I fell on my head, and I got a retina detachment, which means that you get blind. And this is-- as far as I know, I was the first or second person where the retina attachment worked. But it was very different when they-- four weeks immobilized in a hospital. And there are all kinds of stories. But I have to say, my colleagues would come day after day and-- talk about American kindness. You know, they would come and be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, somebody had notified the Red Cross. And I think it's 19-- the end of the war is in '45, isn't it? But I forgot which month. It's before the end of the war that Paul gets to get back to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he came back, and I was still not seeing. I had to always live with something black in front, and little-- not very easy. All kinds of stuff. I have knitted a whole thing with being blind. I'm very proud of that. But anyhow, I, meanwhile, had been speaking all across everywhere, mostly on what my whole life is about. About-- to give dignity to every human being, but also about children and young people that I care about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came once to speak in Minneapolis. That's before Paul came back. And at the University, they invited me. I had no idea, but I didn't know-- they were looking me over, but I didn't know that. So once I wrote at that time to Paul that the two most beautiful places I had seen in the United States were San Francisco and Minneapolis. But I never knew I would get there. So meanwhile, Paul is back. He doesn't have a job yet because, you know, it's not so easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we will stop there. And I'll do that-- do the next one real fast, I hope.
